sections.title: Chairman's Chat
sections.content:
Martin Baxter reports

After 16 years as Club Chairman, and 3 years as sites’ officer before that, I’ve finally run out of steam. I’ll be standing down at the AGM in December. By then I will have chaired 16 AGMs and 96 committee meetings, hosted 15 farmers’ dinners, written 178 chairman’s chats, and had more conversations about the Flying Fund than I care to remember. Most of my 3,380 forum posts have been concerned with the running of the club. With help from the committee I’ve steered the club through the COVID-19 restrictions, defeated a bid to impose controlled airspace over part of the Dales, digitised the sites guide, ring fenced the Flying Fund, and attended the funerals of 2 members.

It’s time for somebody else to take the reins and we could really do with some new blood. The Chairperson doesn’t act alone and we have a very capable committee who just need a bit of coordination and direction. We currently hold 5 committee meetings each year at the Red Lion in Burley in Wharfedale, organised by our very capable Club Secretary.  I’m not going anywhere: I promise to provide a comprehensive handover and be available to provide advice (not interference) on request.

Pilots can be a selfish bunch at times.  ‘I just want to go flying’, but there is a lot that goes on behind the scenes: maintenance of flying sites and our good relationships with farmers, coaching, club nights, reserve repack, first aid, the Flyin, trophies and mugs, library, Skywords, weather station, website and forum.

Stef has also announced that he’ll be standing down, as Social Secretary, at the AGM, so that’s another option for you.

It’s up to you. Are you willing to put something back into the club, as I was 19 years ago?

If you want to find out more about standing for any post on the committee, without making any commitment whatsoever, then please drop me a line: chairman@dhpc.org.uk

sections.title: Social - Last Club Night This Thursday ‘til Post Season
sections.content:
Come to the Horse and Farrier in Otley this Thursday evening 7:30pm for a discussion of paragliding apps

Last chance to enjoy a club night before we’re all too busy just paragliding all the time because the weather is so amazing!

sections.title: Coaching
sections.content:
Pete Logan, Chief Coach reports


100km or Bust!

For those that weren’t able to catch it, I did a talk at the last club night called 100km or Bust. It was a guide to putting together long flights focussed on the Dales. Chris Timson was able to nicely edit it so you can watch it here…
Part 1: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BcdSm_sDp3k 

Part 2: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Njvqozf48FI 
I’ll be repeating it at the Pennine Club night on the 14th April at the Sea View Inn, Chorley. Take the exit off the M61 when you see the gleaming spire of the Preston Mormon Temple.

subsections.title: New Coaches
subsections.content:
A big welcome to three new coaches from last weekend’s training down at The Mynd:

Jake Herbert from Grassington needs little introduction, he’s been an unofficial guide to many of us for Cross Country and safe flying for tens of years already. His flying is national level good and he’s even appeared at the odd PWC. He’ll be spending the year off flying which makes him more available for coaching - MAKE USE OF HIM!

Mike Barker is newer to flying and taking his Pilot’s exam this spring. He’s a paramotor pilot too, so can provide some cross over advice. He’s also a department lead in teaching Physics at a school so is well versed in coaching to begin with.

Jordan Whittaker is similarly new to flying but progressing quickly. Already a pilot, he’s started trainee instructing with Dean at active edge, so he may not be available as much as the other coaches. He’s an ex aeronautical engineer too so his theory knowledge should be up to scratch.

Use the Forum to get in contact: https://www.dhpc.org.uk/forum/viewtopic.php?t=2977 

It’s not just CPs that can benefit from coaching, particularly with Jake joining the team plus some of our other experienced fliers, topics like thermal hunting, route choice, advanced pilot task witnessing are all possible.


This is our current map of coaches’ locations, so there’s someone near you for friendly advice or organising your pilot / AP exams….

subsections.title: Prep
subsections.content:
As the flying season starts it is time to get preparing. I’ve got a few things on my mind which I thought I’d share, hopefully it jogs a few things for you as well.

  • After a winter of less activity and work travel, plus wearing winter kit I am not inside the weight range for my glider. Steps are being taken!
  • My hand temperature when flying is an ongoing project. I suffer from the cold more than average but also want to keep enough dexterity for thermalling and instruments. I’ve ditched my poor quality electric glove batteries and made my own. I’ll be taking a tip from Jim G about putting my brake muffs on my arm rolled up then unrolling them up to cover my hands in flight - potentially a lot safer than using brake muffs the way they’re intended to be used. I’ll be keeping well fed in flight to keep up the heat and taking a few paracetamol beforehand.
  • Tree kit. Not a necessity in the UK unless you’re very unlucky but I’ll be flying in North Macedonia this summer so I’ll be prepping with 30m line, sling and crabs, knife, whistle and my existing phone and Garmin InReach. Where will you be flying that’s out of the ordinary and what will you need or it?

This might be too much but you get the picture:

sections.title: Sites and News
sections.content: Stephen Craven, Southern Sites Officer reports

subsections.title: Windbank
subsections.content:
We have changed the parking instructions for Windbank.  I have updated the site guide on our website

(https://www.dhpc.org.uk/site-guide/hawkswick-windbank-knipe-scar).

Please could everyone park at the lower end of the field from now on and keep well away from the woods at the top end as this is a sensitive area in the shooting season.  Thanks for your cooperation.

subsections.title: Kettlewell
subsections.content: I am pleased to announce that we now have permission to land at the Cricket pitch and neighbouring field to the North West at Kettlewell. I have updated the site guide.

sections.title: Sheep Farming and Free Flight
sections.content:
I’m posting this article written by Christina Richards (originally for the Derbyshire Soaring Club), not only because it’s a great read, but also because it’s very important that all of us understand the relationship between our pastime, and the lives and livelihoods of those working the land.  We had originally considered splitting it into parts and posting at different times of the year, but Christina points out that it works ‘as a single farming cycle piece - the fact it is a year’s work is key’.   It’s therefore a long read, but please do take the time to do so.   Tom Shuttleworth - Sites Officer (North)

As free flight pilots we spend a lot of time in the  countryside, and in Britain that often means sheep farming country with its rolling hills and  helpfully smooth close-cropped grass.  Unfortunately, there's long been tension between farmers who work hard over long hours to wring an increasingly meagre income from the land on the one hand, and passionate free flight pilots who only want to enjoy our beautiful countryside in a harmless way on the other. This need not be so, for with a little thought on both sides we can coexist more easily.

As I work on a sheep farm some of the time, I spend a good deal of time educating farmers away from  misconceptions about free flight and giving tips on how farmers can better enjoy our wonderful sport. This  brief guide is my modest attempt to do the same for free flight pilots regarding farming. I hope it is of some  help in assisting pilots to make choices which are inconsequential for us - such as picking one of two equally  viable fields to land in - but which for farmers may make the difference between profit and loss for the year.

Firstly, do remember that farms are by and large private land and even where they are on access land that does not apply to using them as flying sites. With that said, we fly unpowered aircraft and must sometimes make landings on private land when no other opportunity is available. Very often the best landing fields in  such circumstances are on sheep farms so it is important to know a little of what goes on there.

subsections.title: Tupping
subsections.content:
The farming year starts with tupping which is traditionally held on bonfire night. This is where the tup (ram or male sheep) is finally let in with the ewes (female sheep) to make baby sheep (lambs). He has been really very keen for some weeks by this point and will be both vigorous and remarkably quick with his attentions. He will have had his chest painted with raddle (an oily paint) or had a block of paint strapped to him so that when he covers (mounts) the ewe he will leave a mark and we will know which ewes he has covered and which he has not. After a week or so we will switch colours so we know which ewes will lamb (we say ‘lamb’ to mean give birth as well as for the baby sheep themselves) earlier and which will have lambs later on. Any which are not covered are separated as they may be unwell, and the pregnant ones will get extra feed. A single tup may cover fifty ewes and so gets absolutely knackered - as you  might imagine. He will lose condition (weight and muscle) which he will need to put on again over the year  ready for next year’s tupping. Once he has done covering all the ewes he will be separated to live with  castrated male sheep until next year.

Once the ewes are done they will be left to eat grass, or hay and ‘cake’ (cake is sheep feed a bit like Special K) if it is cold, while the lambs develop. After about 80 days the ewes will be scanned with an ultrasound to see how many lambs they have. Lowland ewes - such as those you will find on the grass of the Peak District - usually have twins or less commonly a large single lamb. There can be triplets or, rarely, quads, however. The ewes on the  moors are different breeds from the lowland ewes and usually have a single lamb as there is less to eat and  the living is harder up there. Hopefully there are no barren ewes, although there are always one or two. When  we know how many lambs each ewe has we mark them with a different colour spray and then split them so  we can feed them extra depending on the number. The reason we put the ewes to the tup around bonfire night is because 147 days later they will lamb onto the spring grass in the sunshine and so will not need extra  care or shelter.

For our purposes as pilots there is little to be aware of during tupping. However there are a couple of things  to note. First, the tups are 120 kilogrammes of four-wheel-drive testosterone-fuelled libidinous rage. While  sheep are mostly docile, at this time of year the tups are not. Being rammed by charging tup (they are also  called rams, recall) can break bones - avoid them! If you do find yourself in a field with a tup give him an  extremely wide berth and do not approach either him or the ewes in the field.

The other important thing to be aware of is that it takes some time for the pregnancy to ‘take’. Ewes will abort  if they are shocked; for example if a paraglider lands among them. Please do not do it if it can in any way be  avoided. When sheep abort they tend to find a quiet spot away from the rest of the flock to do so. This means  that they may appear to be ‘fine’ to the paraglider who lands and then packs up, but they are not. This is  different to making an approach to an established launch or leaving an established landing field, of course. If  you are approaching a flock on a footpath they may well be used to people passing by and be quite happy.

subsections.title: Lambing
subsections.content:
Lambing traditionally happens in March, although the date will, of course, depend on when the farmer decided to put the tup in with the ewes. Lambing is both the best and hardest time of year for sheep farmers. It is the time when all of your hard work feeding the sheep every day there is not enough grass, driving your quad through rain and dark and sleet and  

snow, checking feet and treating with medicine as necessary, moving sheep to the best field (which may, in the depths of winter, still be a mud and frost choked quagmire), and generally  caring for your flock no matter how tired or unwell you are - finally pays off.

It can be a magical time in the lambing shed bringing new life into the world with little bouncing lambs smelling  of wool and milk. With that said, when you have multiple births one after the other, or even at the same time,  after 15 or 20 hours in the icy cold and damp it can be utterly exhausting. For those people who are lambing  on the moors without even the benefit of a lambing shed it is extraordinarily hard work. Farmers at this time are understandably absolutely frazzled. Think of your very hardest day at work and then imagine someone on  their holiday interrupting you; even with a perfectly reasonable request. This can be why farmers are not  always approachable at this time - do try to cut them some slack if you can as they may not be behaving in the way they normally would during the rest of the year down the pub, say. Even the very best of us can get  properly grumpy when we've been up for 20 hours, and especially if things have not been going well.

Very pregnant ewes should not be made to run. When we bring them in to check them, or bring them into the  lambing shed to lamb, we do so extremely gently. Imagine asking a large group of women who are just about  to give birth to move from one building to another. We try to give the ewes a similar level of gentle attention.  Now imagine if you landed your paraglider, quite unexpectedly, among that group of very pregnant women - and the response you would get. Pregnant ewes feel much the same way, so it is really important that we do  not startle them.  

As noted above, pregnant ewes who are startled can abort; and when pregnant ewes are just about to give  birth they are at a high risk of doing so. The particular issue with sheep farming is that if a number of ewes abort there is no option to start again. When my work requires me to write on my computer, if I make a  mistake I can just start again with relatively little loss. In sheep farming you have one single opportunity for  the whole year. If that is lost because lambs die either before being born or afterwards all your work since October is lost, and there is no opportunity to start again. Because margins are so tight in sheep farming even  a small loss of lambs can mean the profit for a whole year is gone. Imagine you had worked on a project at  work for a whole year and someone on their holiday came along and deleted it entirely such that you couldn't  retrieve it. That is how farmers feel about it when we lose their lambs.

For this reason, farmers we have good relationships with, and who allow us to launch and land on their property at all other times of the year, will often close fields they need for pregnant ewes and new lambs during the lambing period. Do check your club website and social media feeds at this time to check for closures. If we break those agreements for the sake of an afternoon's flying, even for a really good XC day, farmers may well (and totally understandably) have to have a total ban on flying from their land because we may have destroyed their income for the year. Again these shocked sheep may look ‘fine’ to us because as paragliders  we do not see the little bodies under the hedges or in the barns where the farmers find them.

I mention baby lambs as well as pregnant ewes because baby lambs are extremely cute and very very stupid  for the first week or so after being born. While they can walk after an hour (it's extraordinary to see), they  really don't have much of an idea of what's going on; and some ewes are not great mums and will wander off.  Baby lambs can also get drunk on milk and will just collapse on their side in the sunshine after a big feed. It's really important to avoid them at these times. Quite often very pregnant ewes and very new lambs will be ‘in by’ (close to the farmhouse or farm buildings) so the farmer can keep an eye on them. For this reason, when we are landing, fields empty of stock (animals) are, of course, best; but if they are not available try to pick one away from buildings.

Lastly, do go and see a lambing if you have the opportunity. It is the most wonderful part of the farming cycle and a joyous thing to behold.

subsections.title: Hay making
subsections.content:
The next part of the farming year is haymaking. This is where grass has been left to grow and is cut to make  feed for the autumn and winter when grass no longer grows (the temperature needs to be consistently above about 11°C for grass to grow). Opening a hay bale in the dark and wet of midwinter is wonderful, as in the  centre it still smells of warm summer days. Once the grass has grown long enough to make hay the weather  needs to be dry for about five days in order for the hay to be cut and laid out in rows which are ‘tedded’  (turned over) once or twice a day to dry properly. After three or four days the hay will be dry and can be  gathered and tied into bales which are stored in a barn. This is why you suddenly see all the farmers out during  a dry spell in summer, working late into the night with tractor lights on so they can get the hay in while it is  dry.

For our purposes as pilots, it is worth being aware that grass meadows which are ready for cutting have high  grass which needs to be standing in order to be cut. If we land on them and compress the grass it will not be  able to be cut and this can mean that the farmer does not have enough feed for their flock in the winter, or at  least a loss of income. Unfortunately from the air one green field looks much like another and so it is not really  possible for us to decide not to land in a haymeadow which has not yet been cut until it is too late. If you do  find yourself in one ideally bunch your glider and make as little compression of the grass as possible to get out  of the field before packing up.

The rows of cut hay are also really vulnerable to us. If the grass is knocked out of the row it has been laid in,  then the bailer (the machine which makes the hay bales) will not be able to pick it up and tie it into a bale. This  means that there is less hay to feed the animals and cut grass will be left on the field which is - strange I know - actually bad for the field because patches of rotten grass stop the cut grass from regrowing.

With that said, fields which have been bailed will often still have the hay bales in them because the process of moving  

them to be stored is less important than cutting them while the weather is dry. Fields with hay bales (or plastic wrapped haylage bales) can be spotted from very high up and make good landing options because the space in between the bales will have been cut and will not be needed again for some months. This makes it a nice even field without even long grass in it. It’s usually fairly flat as it needs to be for the machines to make hay. So it’s good for us for landing and it should also be the least problematic for the farmer. Happily such fields  are easily spottable even from great height and will usually have some sort of road access as farmers will wish  to get farm vehicles in, which means we can easily walk out for the retrieve.

subsections.title: Final Thoughts
subsections.content:
Once the hay has been made and stored ready for the winter all that is left for the farming year is for the  lambs, which now look like ordinary sheep, to be taken to market in the autumn. The care and attention of  the year’s work comes down to the one moment when the livestock auctioneer brings the hammer down and  you know how much your work has brought you. Where once it was said you could pay the mortgage on your  farm from the wool alone, now farmers are struggling to make ends meet from all of their sheep put together  - a way of life which predates the coming of the Romans. After that there is a brief ‘rest’ when you do all of  the other jobs needed on a working farm, and then the cycle starts again with tupping just a few weeks later  in the autumn.

Hopefully this has been a brief and helpful overview of what all the people around us are doing when we sit  on launch waiting for the weather. There are just a few last comments which may help relations further. Firstly,  even when not in the middle of lambing, farmers tend to be quite taciturn because they spend a lot of time  on their own working hard. Even when they're in a good mood they can seem a bit abrupt and grumpy but  don't take this to heart as most of them are really lovely people who care deeply for their families, friends, and the land they work. If you do land in someone’s field do be kind to them, maybe offer them a little  something, and you may end up with a cup of tea or even a lift. There will always be some people who think  that we shouldn't fly at all and that land is theirs and only theirs, even when there is a footpath or the land has  been left for everyone's use. Loud as they can be, these people do not represent farmers any more than the  worst of us represent free fight pilots.  

To make sure that we don't upset people who would otherwise be our friends it is helpful to have the  information above (thank you so much for reading) and also to know the Countryside Code so you do not do  things like climbing over a dry stone wall - which seems like such a trivial thing to do but could cause stock to  get loose and cause a nightmare for a farmer; and will certainly mean an afternoon lost to patching the wall shortly afterwards which should have been spent moving forwards instead of standing still. Similarly, we are  mostly pretty able bodied (and the wonderful free flight community is great at helping people who are not) so  it doesn't hurt to park 20 metres from a gate and walk that trivial extra distance rather than saving that  inconsequential effort and meaning that a farmer loses an afternoon’s work because they cannot turn their  farm vehicle and trailer into their field.  

If farmers don't see us, or take the time because they are working elsewhere, to say thank you for doing these  small actions then please let me say so now. Thank you. Thank you so much. These small things like avoiding  working fields where possible, walking to a field gate to exit, or parking a short distance further away from a  road gate, make such a difference when you are working on a farm and are tired but still need to look after  your animals.

There is much more to sheep farming - it is an endlessly interesting field - and I will be really happy to talk to  you about it on the hill if you have any questions. With just a few tweaks to the way we treat the land around  us we can make sure that, with very little inconvenience to ourselves, we get on with the people who work  the land and, in many cases, have been the guardians of it for generations.

Christina Richards

sections.title: Club Safety Card
sections.content:
Tam reports

The club website is full of useful information. Ugly mug photos of committee members so you know who to abuse on the hill, site guides and update information and any restrictions on them, the club constitution etc etc. It also has safety information, how to handle an incident, and some first aid instruction. It is well worth a good look through on a regular basis.  Included in the safety page is a link to the club safety card, a handy credit card sized document that you can print out, which includes basic info on calling emergency services after a flying incident and has the grid references of each main site take off, should there be an incident. A handy thing to have when the chips are down. With the spread of some services preferring What3words as a location identifier, we have recently added the W3W addresses to the card also, it now looks like this:

Since the card was first developed, mobile apps have of course developed considerably, and it is very easy to have apps that give you your grid reference, and W3W addresses. Given that most accidents won’t happen at t/o, you are strongly advised to get both a grid ref app and W3W and keep them in your flying folder on your mobile. Rather than printing the safety card, you could also simply download that of course and keep it in the same folder. Another piece of flying equipment that I hope I may never have to use, but worth having just in case.

Tam

sections.title: Library
sections.content: Book review by Melise Harland.  We’ve got 2 copies of this book, both out at the moment but if you drop me a line I’ll reserve a copy for you.

subsections.title: Paragliding: From Beginner To Cross-Country
subsections.content:
By: David Sollom and Matthew Cook

This book has 128 pages divided into 8 chapters covering from basic techniques to competition and tandem flying.  It also includes three short appendices, a glossary and further reading suggestions.  All of the figures and photographs are in black and white except for a few pages in the centre which are entirely glossy colour photos. My initial impression was that in format and content it is very much like Touching Cloudbase, which has the advantage of having been updated several times.  

I had mixed feelings about this book but on the plus side it gives simple descriptions which aren’t too technical allowing me at least to understand a couple of things I hadn’t quite grasped before.  There are some really useful tips for novice pilots on controlling the wall on take off and for trouble shooting forward and reverse launches. I also liked some of the tips for cross-country flying and what to look for to find lift as you try to stay in the air and using the clouds to their best advantage.  Some of this I’m sure will be second nature to the seasoned pilots among us but is very useful for those just beginning to edge away from the comfort of the hill.

On the negative side it sometimes over simplifies, making it confusing and unclear.  They don’t always refer to the figures in the text so you get random ones that you have to work out for yourself. I think there are some quite dangerous recommendations in here too like “it is possible for you to use your speed system stirrup soon after take off to help you get back into your harness” and the suggestion that cloud flying is good because you get two or three times faster climbs.  I have always been told not to go near my speed bar low down due to the increased possibility of collapses. Although the authors do give caveats I wouldn’t really want to be sucked into a cloud for several reasons, including disorientation and risk of collision. But hey! They say that if you stay in radio contact with each other and thus keep good vertical separation it's fine, so that’s OK then!

I very much liked the section on meteorology and what to look for on Northern Hemisphere synoptic charts to predict different flying conditions in the UK. I think a lot of the rest of this section is out of date now with the major use of the likes of RASP these days, as is the instruments section.  

There is a nice easy to read air law appendix at the back, but again I suspect this is out of date on some things now.  If you’re as bad as me at remembering acronyms the glossary may also be a useful crib sheet.

I found it hard going in some places and it took a while to get into.  It is however easy to read as it has short sections and you can dip in and out of it, which is a bonus.  Overall I think it was worth the read for the few really useful tips I got out of it.

sections.title: From the Archive
sections.content: Martin Baxter provides some background to our current weather station from 1991.  The club eventually sold Hamish to Rod Buck (father of current BHPA Chair) who replaced it with a Wendy Windblows station with paid telephone access.  It worked well for a number of years but the network fell into disrepair when Rod kept falling off ladders (!).  With financial assistance from Active Edge; Trev Birkbeck and Simon Tomlinson led a project to erect our existing Holfuy setup in 2021.  Liam Toothill is the current ‘Station Master’.

sections.title: Safety
sections.content: Clive Sury reports

subsections.title: Crash Test Dummies
subsections.content:
On Saturday 15th February Cumbria Soaring Club held a special event, namely Crash Test Dummies.  Select pilots shared their incident experiences, causes, actions taken, and lessons learned.  The intention being for the audience to understand what can lead to incidents, how to minimise the risk of occurrence to themselves, and if not, how best to cope in the event of such mishaps.  

 

David Ashcroft started the day by reminding everyone that despite ‘it won’t happen to me’ being a common attitude of most pilots to have, each of us have a ‘target on our back’.  Therefore, with humility it pays to ignore any inner voice of ‘it was a D wing’, ‘I wouldn’t have flown there’ or ‘I wouldn’t have done that’ and instead focus on what has happened to others and accept that it could happen to oneself.  Sobering stuff but with the intent of being informative and instructional for all of us in attendance, and not just for low airtime pilots.  

 

What follows is a precis of what the pilots relayed as their incident stories.  The pilots’ experience ranged from club pilots through to world competition pilots.  Indeed, there were some highly experienced pilots relaying their events and it was highly educational hearing how they coped with the situations they faced.  I haven’t stated the names of the pilots but have noted the locations which may help any who’ve also flown these sites to picture the incidents.  

 

1.            Ager – Reserve thrown: Directly causing a dislocated shoulder

 

The background was that the pilot was attending a competition with some strong punchy thermals earlier in the week and a few reserves thrown already.  At the same time as the pilot went to use his radio (which was handheld setup on the right side), the glider was hit hard by a thermal and it went into an autorotation (spiral). He was about to clear the rotation but then got a twist, lost control and threw the reserve.  The reserve was a square shaped type with the pilot citing this as a factor for a more stable descent (mentioning that round reserves being more inclined to oscillate with the wing still in the air).

 

However, the purpose of this incident is to alert others of the dangers of the reserve bridle catching under the throwing arm, which happened in this case, leading to a dislocated shoulder.  

 

Lessons learnt:

·         Radio setup – better to have a press to talk on one’s hand or radio helmet, as opposed to having to release a brake handle to use a handheld mic.  Releasing a brake handle to use the radio meant the pilot couldn’t initiate inputs to the wing that may have stopped the auto rotation earlier.

·         Come off the speedbar as soon as wing goes into auto rotation (or any other event).

·         Advised that when throwing your reserve to ‘chuck and tuck’.  That is to pull one’s arms and legs IN to avoid getting a limb guillotined when the bridle suddenly comes under tension.  

·         If the pilot is rotating and is unsure of which direction to throw their reserve, then throw at their feet since they are most likely to be in a direction away from the glider.

 

2.            Clough Head – Reserve thrown: Broken arm

 

Flying a new to them 2-liner glider, aiming for a field coming over trees.  The pilot judged that they were a bit high and chose to apply Big Ears.  However, with it being a new glider, the pilot unwittingly had pulled the B lines on a 2-liner glider, initiating in the glider stalling, to which the pilot put ‘arms up’ and the glider lurching forward to then be below the horizon.  As very low, pilot threw the reserve.  After landing the pilot realised their arm was dangling beside their body and transpired it was broken.  Again, here the pilot thinks that this was caused by the reserve bridle catching under his arm when it deployed.

 

Lesson learnt:

·         Don’t assume that ‘hands-up’ is the answer to getting your wing to fly normally again.

·         Know your glider – when flying a new-to-you glider, ensure that you know how to initiate maneuvers as specifically required for it.

·         ‘Chuck & Tuck’ (as above incident 1)

·         Reserve worked with approximately 80 ft of clearance.  It’s never too low to throw. 

·         Questionable whether deploying Big Ears when coming into land and low to the ground is a good idea.  The glider is certified only when flying and any maneuver outside of this means that the flying capabilities are restricted.  

 

3.            Slovenia – Tree Landing: Minor injuries (bruising)

 

Flying behind another glider, hit turbulence leading to loss of height and hit a tree. The wing was wrapped around the top of a tree, 120 ft above the ground, 2500 meters up the mountain and 7 Km from a road.  Pilot radioed the competition team who advised to wait in the tree, however, the pilot climbed down – evaluating that they were near the tree trunk with stable branches.  

 

Lessons learnt:

·         Have a tree rescue kit:  Thin webbing with a carabiner to attach the harness to trunk or main branch to secure the pilot; whistle; reel and weight (30m length of fishing line or dyneema to drop down to pull up a rescue rope); multitool (knife).

·         If landing in the tree, aim for the middle and protect your face, groin and stomach.

·         Don’t move if unsure of the stability of position within the tree.

·         Commercial tree rescue kits available from £60-£80

 

Note: Some harnesses have whistles in the chest strap and the Supair Delight 4 harness has a tree line and a carabiner within it.

 

4.            Dunnerdale Fells – Dragged by reserve

 

The pilot was flying, then experiencing feet going up, lines collapsed, spinning with lines twisted.  Response being to throw the reserve.  On landing the strong wind reinflated the reserve and dragged the pilot back up the mountain approximately 800m!  He only stopped when he hooked over a rock near the top of the ridge.  A flying buddy landed and ran up, grabbing the wing’s lines and pulling in the reserve.

 

Lesson learnt:

·         Have a bridle knife at hand to cut through the lines if required.

 

5.             Dunnerdale Fells - Top landing in a moderate-fresh wind

 

When the pilot top landed the wing twisted and dragged the pilot. Fortunately, the lines became entangled over a rock narrowly preventing the pilot from being dragged over a cliff.

 

Lessons learnt:

·         Don’t top land in the compression  

·         Always have a hook knife to cut lines at hand.

 

6.            Clough Head - Hitting the hill

 

From this pilot’s own words – ‘accidents happen for a reason, or often a multiple of reasons.  In this incident a relatively new club pilot (2 years) crashed into the hill attempting a 360 turn that was evidently too low.  

 

The pilot admitted that their focus was on thermalling with the ambition of doing 360 turns to gain height.  He recalled initially soaring the ridge but when finding more lift he decided to 360 turn. On the 1st or 2nd 360 he might have had a wing tip collapse and couldn’t complete the turn before hitting the ground. His harness took the initial blow but as his canopy dived forward, he impacted a rock and suffered a badly broken leg.  In summary their 360 turn was too low and too close to the hill.

 

Other cautionary factors to be aware of were that the pilot admitted to feeling tired from having already flown a lot that day.  Plus, that they were in the early-intermediate phase of learning a new activity and in their case, over-confident with their ability versus skill and awareness required for what they were attempting to do.  

 

Lessons learnt:

·         Recognise one’s energy levels re tiredness and assess if ‘fit to fly’.

·         Listen to one’s inner voice and spot the unfamiliar.  This pilot felt tired but still walked back up the hill to have another go.  With subsequent lower levels of concentration negatively impacting decision making.

·         Question what trying to achieve and why, and assess constantly if feasible in conditions, environment and ability.

·         Initiate turns away from the hill.

·         Even if achieving a first 360 turn, reassess and adjust before attempting the next 360 etc.

 

7.            Texas - Not checking lines sufficiently

 

The audience was shown the YouTube video posted by Anthony Vella below.  Clear lessons here regarding the need to check one’s lines before flying and whilst in the air.  Watching the footage his wing is evidently not quite symmetrical before complete disaster.  

 

YouTube video:

The Missing Crash Evidence 

 

8.            Ingleborough – back injury after wing collapse

 

This pilot had flown approximately 180 hours in their first 2-3 years of flying.  Flying a High B wing in a familiar environment on what was perceived to be a fine weather day and good lift to cloud base.  

 

The pilot was flying in front of Ingleborough above the rocks near its top.  They turned to topland above the rocks, but the wing collapsed, and they fell landing onto the rocks. Their first message on the radio was ‘Crash, Crash!’ but subsequently posted ‘I think I’m ok’.  However, another pilot on hearing this, didn’t like the pilot’s tone of voice and went to investigate.  Despite feeling very sore from the crash, the pilot chose to walk down the hill with the assistance of the first helping pilot.  Another pilot also heard about the incident and walked up the hill to also help – taking over from the first helper who walked down.  Until now the injured pilot insisted that they could make it down the hill.  After 2 ½ hours of slow progress the pilot couldn’t walk any further. He still had a long way to get off the hill so the rescue service was called.  The pilot had a broken hip, spine injury and internal bleeding.  They were kept warm wrapped within a glider whilst waiting for mountain rescue and thereafter taken to A&E.  

 

Lessons learnt:

·         The second helping pilot mistakenly assumed that they would just be assisting walking someone down the hill, leaving any equipment for first aid at the car.  They reacted straight away without pausing to consider what might be useful as aids.

·         The injured pilot’s adrenalin kicked in, with the initial insistence on being able to walk down the hill.  A fall from height onto rocks should be a strong signal to call for mountain rescue.  The risk of internal bleeding injuries is severe and needs to be considered regarding the need for assistance.

·         Don’t trust the pilot if they say they're OK.  Do your own assessment and initiate support services and responses as one considers required, outside of what an injured pilot may state (noting their adrenalin and thought processes may be clouding their judgement).

·           It is advisable to contact emergency services (mountain rescue) as soon as possible to ensure the injured pilot receives timely medical attention, considering daylight and the time required to reach A&E.  In this case, the air ambulance could have called, and the pilot would have been attended to immediately upon transfer at A&E.

·         The pilot kept warm through being wrapped up in a wing.  With internal bleeding it is essential to keep warm.  Knowledge of first aid principles of DRABC and how to respond to help is useful through attending first aid courses.

·         In A&E have someone with you.  The pilot had to wait a long time before being treated.  Another person could have helped escalate help in the A&E department sooner.

·         Ensure your partner is kept informed from the first message since they suffer distress through lack of knowledge of how the injured pilot is doing and what is happening.

 

9.            Switzerland – Wing hanging over a cliff edge

 

During a hike and fly competition, the pilot’s legs clipped an electric fence.  The pilot fell down the mountain and ended up hanging on a cliff face.  The launch may also have been compromised by being on what had become the leeward of the mountain.  The pilot noticed the low fence in front of their chosen launch location but saw 2 pilots’ takeoff from the same location.  The pilot chose not to use own judgement in evaluating suitability of the launch area, instead focusing on their haste to launch off the mountain.

 

Lessons learnt:

·         Insurance – read the small print.  In this instance the pilot was too high (>1200m) for their level of insurance which didn’t cover the helicopter.  The pilot had to pay €5,000 for the helicopter!!!!

·         Be 100% clear of obstacles at launch.

 

 

The above is a very brief precis of a few of the incidents shared on the day to help others get the key messages and what to either do or not do to avoid a similar situation happening to them.  David Ashcroft also shares some ‘golden rules’ for safety which were:

 

1.            Don’t fly near the ground when the wind is ‘well off the hill’. Fly away if your ground speed drops to 10 km/h and you’re near the ground. There’s a high chance of turbulence.

2.            Don’t scratch flying down wind (exacerbates impact).

3.            Don’t scratch steep slopes you would bounce down if you accidentally crashed! [When wrapped in a pod harness, this is easy to initiate].

4.            Pull the safety card – Forget about your XC, or personal best, etc. If in a tricky situation, fly out to gain more height immediately.

5.            If you have a life-threatening incident, back-off’ for the rest of the year. Take it as your warning.

6.            Don’t be lazy – e.g., find the safest, cleanest take-off, not the nearest.

7.            Is your head in the right place? If you make 3 errors before taking off, then reassess if you should be taking off right now?

8.            Safety with height – height above the hill is your friend (300 ft plus). Having a serious blow out at say 50-150 will break something. Staying high gives you or your canopy time to recover.

 

 

The day was extremely informative, and I’d recommend anyone to go on a similar event if organised again.  Many of the pilots describing incidents that they had been involved in had considerable experience and expertise.  Ability and focus can help mitigate the chances of accidents and applying lessons learnt from the experience of others is designed to help here.  However, at the end of the day, someone having an incident may not reflect their flying ability, but a combination of events, thought processes and actions leading to an unfortunate outcome.  I’d like to take this opportunity to thank Dave for organising such an insightful day, plus proofreading this article.  And not least thank you to the 9 pilots for sharing their stories in such an open and frank manner.

Safe flying,

Clive.

sections.title: Skew T - For you and me! (Part 1 - the Graph)
sections.content:
Jordan Whittaker reports

I’ll be doing a little series over the next few months talking about all things Skew T Log P! So keep reading…

Firstly a disclaimer, I’m no expert. This series aim is not an in depth study of the chart, but rather it’s to let every day pilots feel comfortable using Skew T’s to predict cloud types, as well as thermal frequency, strength and altitudes. There’s plenty of videos on youtube for anyone wanting an in depth look, and I’m always happy to chat to anyone in person to teach about it or learn more myself! It’s something I’ve started looking at most days, whilst comparing it to the actual sky to get a good understanding. After a while it all sort of clicked!

Firstly, here is an empty Skew T graph! It looks confusing but I’ll go over in simple terms what all the background graph lines mean.

Solid Blue Horizontal Lines

These represent air pressure. They’re in a logarithmic scale (numbers get closer together as they get nearer the bottom) so that as you go up the graph, you go up in equal amounts of altitude (seen on the right axis).

Solid Red Diagonal Lines

These show temperature. I’ve coloured in -20 degrees, -10 and 0 for example. This is why a skew T is called a skew T (Skewed Temperature, Log Pressure).

Dashed Purple Diagonal Lines

Mixing ratio. To keep this simple, this shows how moist the air is. This is important when determining information about thermals (in a later write up!)

Solid Green Diagonal Lines

DALR, dry adiabatic lapse rate. This is the rate that a dry (unsaturated) pocket of air will cool down at as it rises. Useful for thermals!

Dashed Green Lines (Going up and to the left)

SALR, saturated adiabatic lapse rate. This is the rate that a saturated pocket of air will cool down at as it rises. For example once a thermal hits cloud base, the air is now saturated (cloud) and so will cool at a different rate as it continues to rise.

On this graph

 2 lines are drawn in, which is real data collected from a weather balloon every 12 hours (or used to make a forecast Skew T like below).

The blue line shows the dew point,

and the red line shows the environmental lapse rate,

or how the air cools as it rises (or not!). In more simple terms, the red line shows the temperature at that altitude.

For this graph (on the Windy app), the graph lines may be different colours to the original one above, so be aware of that and get your head around which is which. But for now I’m not focussing so much on all the graph’s lines, but just the red and blue data lines showing temperature and dew point. In this example, the blue and red lines never meet, or never really come close to each other, meaning the temperature with altitude in this location never gets close to the dew point, and therefore no clouds form.

This will be a clear blue sky day.

This is made further by the fact there’s a strong inversion at a fairly low altitude. You can see this as the red line, starting at a temperature of around 10 degrees at surface level, cools down to around 1-2 degrees, but then it starts moving to the right, showing…

the air mass is inverted!

It warms up from 1-2 degrees to around 5-6 degrees above, before beginning to cool again.

In this next example, notice how the lines overlap.

In this example, the overlapping lines high up show there will be a lot of high cirrus, cirrostratus by the looks of it! Whenever lines meet like that, there will be

stratus cloud at that level.

Nearer the bottom, at a lower altitude, the lines don’t meet, but they do get close. When the temperature gets close to the dew point, clouds will start forming, but the closer the lines get, and the longer they stay near on overlapping each other, the thicker the cloud will be.

So keep an eye out this month on Skew T’s and try to match the clouds you see to the graph, see if it matches. If you see cirrus, do the lines meet high on the graph? How about mid or low level clouds? Windy is useful as it shades in the expected cloud amount on the side of the graph, cheating a little, but useful!

Next month we’ll dive into learning how to read thermals on the graph. How to tell cloud base, thermal strength at different altitudes and whether a day is at risk of overdeveloping! Stay tuned!

sections.title: PG Tips 🫖 (Paragliding tips)
sections.content:
Martin Baxter reports

I’m just back from a flying trip to Morocco.  An attentive local spotted that one of our group was soaring along the ridge with his reserve handle dangling below his harness.  He was alerted over the radio and came in to land.  It transpired that both pins had come out, and the reserve itself was only being held in by friction.  Had it fallen out it would almost certainly have deployed quite close to the ground with potentially catastrophic consequences.  What would you have done (aside from checking the handle and pins before taking off, of course)?

Later that night, in the bar, I overheard some top advice from a former instructor.  He suggested using the handle to extricate the reserve and then stowing it safely in your jacket/pod/lap.  Whilst pulling the handle in these circumstances requires balls, the reserve shouldn’t deploy unless you drop or throw it.  Counter intuitive, but top advice.
